intro:
Citizenship in the biblical world carried significant legal privileges and social obligations, and the New Testament writers employ the concept in both its Roman civic form and as a theological metaphor. Roman citizenship granted rights to appeal to Caesar, exemption from certain punishments, and legal protections unavailable to provincial subjects. Paul's Roman citizenship, inherited by birth (Acts 22:28), proved decisive at several points in his ministry, enabling him to appeal his case to Rome and shielding him from summary punishment at Philippi and Jerusalem.

The theological dimension of citizenship is equally prominent. Paul describes believers as fellow citizens with the saints and members of God's household (Ephesians 2:19), and reminds the Philippians that their citizenship is in heaven, from which they await the Savior (Philippians 3:20). The Old Testament grounded citizenship in the covenant community: certain categories of persons were excluded from the assembly of Israel (Deuteronomy 23:1), while foreigners who embraced the covenant could be incorporated. This OT pattern anticipates the NT abolition of ethnic barriers in the new covenant community.
